questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of painless swelling in the right side of her neck. She reports drenching night sweats and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg over 2 months. On examination, there is a firm, rubbery, non-tender lymph node in the right supraclavicular region measuring 3 cm. No other nodes palpable. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of painless, rubbery lymphadenopathy with B symptoms (night sweats, weight loss) in a young adult is classic for Hodgkin lymphoma. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma is more common in older patients and often presents with widespread disease. Tuberculous lymphadenitis typically causes matted, sometimes fluctuant nodes and may have systemic symptoms but less specific. Reactive lymphadenopathy lacks B symptoms, and infectious mononucleosis usually has pharyngitis and splenomegaly.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with severe generalised pruritus of 3 months' duration, worse at night, without any rash. He has also noticed occasional low-grade fevers. On examination, a small, firm, left supraclavicular lymph node is palpable (<1 cm). Skin examination is normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Unexplained pruritus, especially when accompanied by fevers and a palpable supraclavicular node, is a classic (though atypical) presentation of Hodgkin lymphoma. Pruritus is a well-recognised B symptom and may precede detectable lymphadenopathy. Atopic dermatitis and scabies would have visible skin changes. Cholestatic pruritus is associated with jaundice or liver disease, absent here. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma less commonly presents with pruritus as an isolated symptom.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with acute onset of facial swelling, distended neck veins, and dyspnoea on lying flat. He reports a 2-month history of night sweats and weight loss. Chest X-ray shows a large anterior mediastinal mass. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO) from an anterior mediastinal mass in a young adult with B symptoms is highly suggestive of lymphoma, most commonly Hodgkin lymphoma in this age group. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma is also possible but typically occurs in older patients or those with HIV. Thymoma rarely presents with SVCO and is not associated with B symptoms. Germ cell tumours may cause mediastinal mass but often have elevated tumour markers. Sarcoidosis usually has bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, not an anterior mediastinal mass.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with fatigue and pallor. He has lost 10 kg over 6 months and has had intermittent fevers. He has no palpable lymphadenopathy. Full blood count shows Hb 90 g/L, WCC 3.2 × 10⁹/L, platelets 100 × 10⁹/L, and a raised ESR of 110 mm/h. Bone marrow biopsy reveals granulomas and Reed-Sternberg cells. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In an older patient with cytopenias, B symptoms, and bone marrow showing Reed-Sternberg cells, the diagnosis is Hodgkin lymphoma. This presentation without peripheral lymphadenopathy is atypical but well-recognised. Myelodysplastic syndrome would not cause B symptoms or granulomas. Aplastic anaemia typically has pancytopenia but no fevers or weight loss. Acute myeloid leukaemia shows blasts. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma rarely involves marrow in this pattern with granulomas.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of left upper quadrant discomfort and early satiety. She has had drenching night sweats and fevers. Examination reveals massive splenomegaly (10 cm below costal margin), but no lymphadenopathy. Full blood count shows mild anaemia and lymphocytosis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Isolated splenomegaly with B symptoms in a middle-aged woman should raise suspicion for Hodgkin lymphoma, which can occasionally present with primary splenic involvement. Chronic lymphocytic leukaemia typically has lymphocytosis and peripheral nodes. Myelofibrosis has a leucoerythroblastic blood film and often splenomegaly but less prominent B symptoms. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma more commonly presents with nodal disease. Sarcoidosis usually has bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and lung involvement.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of painless right-sided neck swelling. She has intermittent fevers and night sweats. Examination reveals a 3 cm, firm, non-tender lymph node in the right supraclavicular region. Blood tests show mild anaemia and elevated ESR. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Excisional biopsy of the lymph node
Core needle biopsy of the lymph node
CT scan of neck, chest, abdomen and pelvis
Fine-needle aspiration of the lymph node
questions.explanation: Excisional biopsy of the entire lymph node is the gold standard for diagnosing Hodgkin lymphoma, as it provides adequate tissue for histology and immunophenotyping. Fine-needle aspiration often yields insufficient cells for definitive diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with classical Hodgkin lymphoma (mixed cellularity subtype) has been diagnosed after an excisional lymph node biopsy. He is asymptomatic apart from a 2 cm left axillary node. Full blood count and LFTs are normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation for staging?
options: CT scan of neck, chest, abdomen and pelvis
questions.explanation: PET-CT is now the standard staging investigation for Hodgkin lymphoma in the UK, as it combines anatomical and functional imaging and can guide biopsy. CT alone would under-stage if there is occult disease.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with stage IIA classical Hodgkin lymphoma undergoes two cycles of ABVD chemotherapy. A PET-CT scan is performed and shows a Deauville score of 4 in a previously involved mediastinal lymph node. What does this result indicate?
options: Complete remission with no residual disease
Partial remission with metabolically active residual disease
Resistant disease requiring immediate change of therapy
Equivocal result that requires a repeat scan in 4 weeks
questions.explanation: A Deauville score of 4 indicates moderate residual uptake higher than liver background, consistent with metabolically active residual disease (partial remission). In most protocols, Deauville 4 is considered a positive scan, but does not necessarily indicate resistance yet.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man presents with bilateral inguinal lymphadenopathy and severe pruritus, but no B symptoms. An excisional lymph node biopsy shows features of nodular lymphocyte-predominant Hodgkin lymphoma. Immunohistochemistry is sent. Which marker is typically positive in this subtype?
questions.explanation: Nodular lymphocyte-predominant Hodgkin lymphoma is a B-cell neoplasm that expresses B-cell markers such as CD20, while classical Hodgkin lymphoma is typically CD15-positive and CD30-positive. PAX5 may be positive in both but less specific.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old man presents with a large mediastinal mass on chest X-ray, found incidentally. CT confirms a 10 cm anterior mediastinal mass. CT-guided core needle biopsy is performed but yields only necrotic tissue and is inconclusive. What is the most appropriate next step to establish a diagnosis?
options: Start empirical chemotherapy for presumed Hodgkin lymphoma
Perform PET-CT to guide a repeat biopsy site
Observe with repeat imaging in 6 weeks
questions.explanation: When a core needle biopsy is inconclusive, an open surgical biopsy (e.g., via mediastinoscopy) is recommended to obtain adequate tissue for histology and immunophenotyping. Starting empirical treatment without a definitive diagnosis is inappropriate.

questions.stem: 28-year-old woman presents with stage IA classical Hodgkin lymphoma, favourable risk (asymptomatic, non-bulky, no B symptoms). What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: ABVD ×2 cycles plus involved site radiotherapy
questions.explanation: For early-stage favourable Hodgkin lymphoma, combined modality therapy with 2 cycles of ABVD followed by involved node radiotherapy is standard. ABVD alone (option 0) is for unfavourable early-stage or advanced; escalated BEACOPP (option 1) is reserved for high-risk advanced disease; radiotherapy alone (option 2) has higher relapse risk.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with stage IIIB classical Hodgkin lymphoma (bulky mediastinal mass, B symptoms). What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Advanced stage classical HL (IIIB/IV) is treated with ABVD chemotherapy, typically 6 cycles. Escalated BEACOPP is more toxic and not first-line in UK guidelines. Brentuximab vedotin is for relapsed/refractory disease. Radiotherapy alone is insufficient for advanced disease.

questions.stem: 24-year-old woman with relapsed classical Hodgkin lymphoma 2 years after ABVD, now with nodal and splenic involvement, ECOG 0. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation from matched sibling
High-dose BEAM chemotherapy with autologous stem cell rescue
questions.explanation: Fit patients with relapsed/refractory HL should receive salvage chemotherapy followed by high-dose therapy (e.g., BEAM) and autologous stem cell rescue. Allogeneic SCT is considered after autologous failure. Brentuximab is used pre-autologous or in non-transplant candidates, but not alone as definitive therapy.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with classical Hodgkin lymphoma (stage IIA) and a history of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (FVC 60% predicted). Which drug is contraindicated in his planned ABVD regimen?
questions.explanation: Bleomycin can cause pulmonary fibrosis, with risk increased in patients with pre-existing lung disease or age >40. Alternative regimens such as AVD (without bleomycin) should be used. Doxorubicin is cardiotoxic but not contraindicated here; vinblastine and dacarbazine are safer.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with nodular lymphocyte predominant Hodgkin lymphoma (NLPHL), stage IA (single cervical node excised). What is the most appropriate first-line management?
questions.explanation: NLPHL is an indolent variant; for early-stage disease (IA/IIA), involved site radiotherapy alone is standard, achieving excellent outcomes. Chemotherapy (ABVD) is reserved for advanced stages. Rituximab is used in relapsed/refractory disease but not first-line.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a 6-week history of a painless, enlarging left supraclavicular lymph node. She is systemically well. Examination confirms a 3 cm firm, rubbery node. No other nodes or hepatosplenomegaly. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange excision biopsy of the node
questions.explanation: Excision biopsy is gold standard for histological diagnosis of suspected Hodgkin lymphoma; imaging is performed after tissue diagnosis for staging, and steroids are avoided before biopsy as they may obscure histology.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with stage IIA Hodgkin lymphoma receiving ABVD chemotherapy develops fever (38.2°C) and a non-productive cough on day 10 of cycle 1. He feels acutely unwell. Observations: HR 105, BP 110/70, SpO2 96% on air. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Check full blood count and neutrophil count
Delay the next cycle of chemotherapy
Refer to the respiratory team for bronchoscopy
questions.explanation: Fever during chemotherapy raises suspicion of neutropenic sepsis; immediate FBC (especially neutrophil count) is required before starting antibiotics to confirm neutropenia.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known stage III Hodgkin lymphoma on ABVD chemotherapy presents with acute onset of facial swelling, distended neck veins, and dyspnoea. She is alert and maintaining oxygen saturations. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give high-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask
Request an urgent CT chest with contrast
questions.explanation: Suspected superior vena cava obstruction due to lymphoma requires immediate steroids to reduce oedema; definitive imaging (CT) should follow but steroids are the priority to relieve obstruction.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man previously treated for Hodgkin lymphoma (complete remission 3 years ago) presents with 6 weeks of fevers, night sweats, and weight loss. He feels fatigued. On examination, there is a new left axillary lymph node. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer for autologous stem cell transplant
Check full blood count and inflammatory markers
Perform an urgent lymph node biopsy
questions.explanation: PET-CT is the recommended first-line investigation for suspected relapse of Hodgkin lymphoma to confirm and stage disease before any treatment is initiated.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman in remission from Hodgkin lymphoma (treated with ABVD and involved-field radiotherapy to the mediastinum 2 years ago) attends with fatigue and palpitations. She is pale. Bloods: Hb 85 g/L, MCV 72 fL, ferritin 8 µg/L. She has no overt bleeding. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start oral ferrous sulphate 200 mg three times daily
Check serum B12 and folate levels
questions.explanation: Immediate management of symptomatic iron deficiency anaemia is oral iron replacement; investigation for a cause (e.g., GI endoscopy) should be arranged but is secondary to treating the anaemia in a stable patient.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with stage II Hodgkin lymphoma undergoing ABVD chemotherapy presents with a fever of 38.5°C and rigors. She has a central line in situ. Which single finding most strongly indicates that this is a medical emergency requiring immediate intervention?
questions.explanation: In neutropenic patients, hypotension (systolic BP <90 mmHg) is a red flag for septic shock requiring immediate antibiotics and fluids. While fever and rigors are concerning, hypotension signifies haemodynamic instability and is the most urgent finding.

questions.stem: An 18-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of a painless neck lump and intermittent fevers. On examination, he has a large supraclavicular lymph node. Which single clinical sign should raise immediate concern for a life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: In a young patient with mediastinal lymphadenopathy, distended neck veins suggest superior vena cava obstruction (SVCO), which can compromise venous return and cause airway oedema. This is a medical emergency requiring urgent imaging and referral.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with nodular sclerosis Hodgkin lymphoma is undergoing chemotherapy. He develops progressive lower back pain and difficulty walking. Which single finding on examination is most concerning for a serious complication requiring immediate investigation?
questions.explanation: Spinal cord compression is an oncological emergency. Urinary retention (or incontinence) is a red flag sign indicating cauda equina or cord involvement, requiring urgent MRI and dexamethasone.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with Hodgkin lymphoma treated with ABVD chemotherapy 4 weeks ago presents with acute dyspnoea and a dry cough. She is afebrile. Which auscultatory finding is most suggestive of a potentially life-threatening treatment-related complication?
questions.explanation: Bleomycin can cause interstitial pneumonitis, which may progress to pulmonary fibrosis. Bilateral basal crackles are characteristic, whereas wheeze suggests bronchospasm, and stridor indicates upper airway obstruction.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man who was treated for Hodgkin lymphoma with MOPP chemotherapy 10 years ago presents with fatigue, bruising, and recurrent infections. Which single finding on full blood count and film is most concerning for a life-threatening late complication?
questions.explanation: Alkylating agents (MOPP) increase risk of secondary acute myeloid leukaemia. Pancytopenia with circulating blasts is diagnostic of leukaemia, a life-threatening complication requiring urgent haematology referral.
